name: Should flooring be installed before or after painting?

mainEntity.name: Should I paint walls before or after flooring installation?
mainEntity.text: Should I paint walls before or after flooring installation?

acceptedAnswer.text: In many projects, painting before flooring installation is the cleaner and faster sequence because it reduces the chance of drips or damage to the new floor. However, the final finish is usually not complete until after the flooring is installed, because baseboards, caulk lines, nail holes, and touch-up paint are often affected by the work. If the flooring project includes baseboard removal, expect to repaint those areas afterward. If the install requires sanding, demolition, or dust-heavy prep, painting after the flooring may be better for certain areas. The right order depends on the scope of work, whether trim is being replaced, and how much surface repair is needed. Ask the contractor how they prefer to sequence the project and whether they include trim reinstallation or only the flooring itself. The goal is to avoid damaging finished surfaces while still getting a clean final look.
acceptedAnswer.description: Painting is often easier before flooring goes in, but final touch-up is usually needed after installation. Many homeowners paint first to avoid splatter and then finish baseboards, caulk, and trim once the new floor is installed.

suggestedAnswer.text: In many projects, painting before flooring installation is the cleaner and faster sequence because it reduces the chance of drips or damage to the new floor. However, the final finish is usually not complete until after the flooring is installed, because baseboards, caulk lines, nail holes, and touch-up paint are often affected by the work. If the flooring project includes baseboard removal, expect to repaint those areas afterward. If the install requires sanding, demolition, or dust-heavy prep, painting after the flooring may be better for certain areas. The right order depends on the scope of work, whether trim is being replaced, and how much surface repair is needed. Ask the contractor how they prefer to sequence the project and whether they include trim reinstallation or only the flooring itself. The goal is to avoid damaging finished surfaces while still getting a clean final look.
